title: Copilot Primitives — Prompts, Instructions, Skills, and Agents

steps.title: Welcome to the Lab
steps.instruction:
Welcome! In this hands-on lab you'll create each of the four Copilot customization primitives: Prompt files, Instruction files, Skills, and Agents.

Follow the steps in this panel — the glowing edge and arrow will show you where to look. By the end, you'll have a working set of customization files in your repo.

steps.title: Explore Your Repo Structure
steps.instruction:
We've cloned a starter project for you and opened it in the Explorer. Take a look at the file tree — it has a small Node/TypeScript app to work with.

We're going to create a .github/ folder with subfolders for each primitive: prompts/, instructions/, skills/, and agents/.

steps.title: Create the Folder Structure
steps.instruction:
Open the integrated terminal and run these commands to create the folder structure:

mkdir -p .github/prompts .github/instructions .github/skills .github/agents

This sets up the conventional layout for all four primitives.
steps.tip: You can ask Copilot: <kbd>@terminal how do I create nested directories?</kbd>

steps.title: Verify With the Explorer
steps.instruction:
Switch back to the Explorer panel and expand the .github/ folder. You should see four empty subfolders.

This structure isn't required — Copilot discovers files by their extension, not their location — but the convention keeps your repo organized and signals intent to your team.

steps.title: Panels Closed
steps.instruction:
We've closed the sidebar for you.

For future reference:
• Explorer: Ctrl+Shift+E
• Terminal: Ctrl+`

steps.title: Create a Test-Generation Prompt
steps.instruction:
We've opened a new file for you. Save it as .github/prompts/generate-test.prompt.md.

Type the following YAML frontmatter and prompt body:

---
mode: ask
description: Generate a unit test for the selected function
tools:
- changes
---

Write a comprehensive unit test for the function in the current selection.

steps.title: Add Requirements to the Prompt
steps.instruction:
Below your prompt body, add formatting instructions:

**Requirements:**
- Use the project's existing test framework
- Cover happy path, edge cases, and error conditions
- Follow the naming convention: test_<function>_<scenario>

The more specific your prompt body is, the more consistent the results will be every time you run it.

steps.title: Create a Documentation Prompt
steps.instruction:
Create a second prompt file and save it as .github/prompts/document-function.prompt.md:

---
mode: ask
description: Generate documentation for the selected code
---

Write clear documentation for the code in the current selection.

**Include:**
- A one-sentence summary
- Parameter descriptions with types
- Return value description
- One usage example

Having multiple prompt files builds a team library of reliable, reusable tasks.
steps.tip: Good prompt libraries grow organically. When someone on the team writes a great prompt, save it as a file and share it.

steps.title: Panels Closed
steps.instruction:
We've closed the editor tab for you.

For future reference:
• New File: Ctrl+N

steps.title: Run From the Prompt Picker
steps.instruction:
Open Copilot Chat using the shortcut Ctrl+Shift+I, or run the command Chat: Open Chat from the Command Palette (Ctrl+Shift+P). In the chat input, type / to open the prompt picker.

You should see both generate-test and document-function in the list. Select generate-test to run it.

steps.title: Run With a Selection
steps.instruction:
For prompts that reference ${selection}, the selected code matters. Write a small function in a file (or use one from the previous course), select it, then run your document-function prompt from the picker.

Notice how the output adapts to the specific code you selected.

steps.title: Compare the Two Prompts
steps.instruction:
Run both prompts on the same function and compare the results. One generates tests, the other generates documentation — same input, different outputs because the prompt body drives the behavior.

This is why prompt files are so powerful: they make a complex, multi-paragraph prompt feel like a one-click action.
steps.tip: You can also run prompts by right-clicking a file in the Explorer → Copilot → Run Prompt.

steps.title: Panels Closed
steps.instruction:
We've closed the sidebar for you.

For future reference:
• Copilot Chat: Ctrl+Shift+I

steps.title: Create Repo-Wide Instructions
steps.instruction:
Create a new file and save it as .github/copilot-instructions.md.

Add your team's coding standards:

# Copilot Instructions

## Language & Framework
- Always use TypeScript with strict mode
- Prefer async/await over callbacks

## Style
- Use single quotes for strings
- Prefer named exports over default exports

steps.title: Add a Testing Section
steps.instruction:
Instruction files work best when they cover multiple dimensions of your team's standards. Add a testing section:

## Testing
- Write tests using Vitest, not Jest
- Every public function must have at least one test
- Use descriptive test names: "should <behavior> when <condition>"

Start small and grow your instructions over time. If you keep correcting the same thing, that correction belongs in this file.

steps.title: Test Your Instructions
steps.instruction:
Open Copilot Chat and ask it to generate some code:

Write a function that fetches user data from an API and returns the parsed response

Check the output carefully: Does it use TypeScript? Async/await? Single quotes? Named exports? Your instructions should be shaping every response now.

steps.title: Try Breaking a Rule
steps.instruction:
Ask Copilot for something that conflicts with your instructions:

Write a function using callbacks to read a file

Even though you asked for callbacks, Copilot should push toward async/await because your instructions say "prefer async/await over callbacks." This is the power of always-on guidance — it shapes output even when the request doesn't explicitly mention your standards.

steps.title: Panels Closed
steps.instruction:
We've closed the sidebar for you.

For future reference:
• New File: Ctrl+N
• Copilot Chat: Ctrl+Shift+I

steps.title: Create Test-Scoped Instructions
steps.instruction:
Create a new file at .github/instructions/testing.instructions.md.

Add an applyTo frontmatter and scoped rules:

---
applyTo: "**/*.test.ts"
---

# Testing Instructions

- Use describe / it blocks (not test)
- Mock external services with vi.mock()
- Name test cases: "should <behavior> when <condition>"

steps.title: Create Component-Scoped Instructions
steps.instruction:
Create another scoped file at .github/instructions/react-components.instructions.md:

---
applyTo: "src/components/**/*.tsx"
---

# React Component Instructions

- Use functional components with hooks (no class components)
- Colocate styles with components using CSS modules
- Export a Props type for every component
- Include accessibility attributes (aria-label, role)

Different file patterns get different rules — testing files get testing rules, components get component rules.
steps.tip: Scoped instructions are additive — they combine with your repo-wide copilot-instructions.md, they don't replace it.

steps.title: Understand the Scoping
steps.instruction:
Ask Copilot to explain the difference:

When I'm editing a .test.ts file, which instruction files does Copilot load? What about when I'm editing a .tsx component?

The answer: repo-wide instructions load always. Scoped instructions load only when the active file matches the applyTo pattern. Both sets combine when applicable.

steps.title: Panels Closed
steps.instruction:
We've closed the sidebar for you.

For future reference:
• New File: Ctrl+N
• Copilot Chat: Ctrl+Shift+I

steps.title: Create an API Design Skill
steps.instruction:
Create a new file at .github/skills/api-patterns/SKILL.md.

Write a skill with YAML frontmatter containing the description, followed by the knowledge payload:

---
description: Use this skill when creating REST API endpoints.
---

# API Design Skill

## Patterns
- Use Express Router for route grouping
- Return consistent JSON: { data, error, meta }
- Validate input with zod schemas
- Handle errors with middleware, not try/catch in routes

The description field in the frontmatter is what agents use to decide when to invoke this skill.
steps.tip: Skills use subfolders (like api-patterns/SKILL.md) because the folder name becomes the skill's identifier and you can include supporting files alongside the SKILL.md. See the Copilot customization spec at code.visualstudio.com/docs/copilot/copilot-customization for details.

steps.title: Create a Database Skill
steps.instruction:
Create a second skill at .github/skills/database/SKILL.md:

---
description: Use this skill when writing database queries or migrations.
---

# Database Patterns Skill

## Patterns
- Use Prisma as the ORM
- Always use parameterized queries (never string concatenation)
- Wrap multi-table operations in transactions
- Name migrations descriptively: add_user_email_index

Multiple skills let agents draw on the right knowledge for the right task.

steps.title: Instructions vs. Skills
steps.instruction:
Ask Copilot Chat:

What's the difference between a .instructions.md file and a SKILL.md file in Copilot?

Key insight: Instructions are always loaded into every conversation. Skills are loaded on demand when an agent recognizes the current task matches the skill's description. Skills keep the context window efficient by only appearing when relevant.
steps.tip: If a rule should apply everywhere ("use TypeScript"), make it an instruction. If knowledge is deep but situational ("how to deploy to Azure"), make it a skill.

steps.title: Panels Closed
steps.instruction:
We've closed the sidebar for you.

For future reference:
• New File: Ctrl+N
• Copilot Chat: Ctrl+Shift+I

steps.title: Create a Code Reviewer Agent
steps.instruction:
Create a new file at .github/agents/code-reviewer.agent.md.

Define the agent with frontmatter and instructions:

---
description: Reviews code for quality and best practices
tools:
- changes
- codebase
---

# Code Reviewer

You are a senior engineer reviewing code. Focus on:
1. Readability and naming
2. Error handling
3. Performance concerns
4. Test coverage gaps

Be constructive. Suggest improvements, don't just criticize.

steps.title: Refine the Agent's Persona
steps.instruction:
A good agent has a clear output format. Add this to the bottom of your agent file:

## Output Format

For each finding:
- **Severity:** Critical / Warning / Suggestion
- **Location:** File and line reference
- **Issue:** What you found
- **Fix:** How to resolve it

End with a summary table of all findings.

The more specific the persona, the more consistent the agent's output.

steps.title: Create a Documentation Agent
steps.instruction:
Create a second agent at .github/agents/docs-writer.agent.md:

---
description: Generates and maintains project documentation
tools:
- codebase
- changes
---

# Documentation Writer

You are a technical writer. When asked to document code:
1. Read the source code thoroughly
2. Write clear, jargon-free explanations
3. Include usage examples
4. Note any prerequisites or gotchas

Match the project's existing documentation style.

Each agent is a virtual team member with a distinct role and expertise.

steps.title: Wire Instructions to an Agent
steps.instruction:
Open your code-reviewer.agent.md and add an instructions field to the frontmatter:

---
description: Reviews code for quality and best practices
instructions:
- .github/instructions/testing.instructions.md
tools:
- changes
- codebase
---

The instructions field explicitly wires instruction files to this agent. When invoked, the agent always loads those guidelines on top of your repo-wide instructions.

steps.title: Explore Available Tools
steps.instruction:
The tools field controls what an agent can do. Common tools include:

changes - see file diffs
codebase - search the project
fetch - read URLs
terminal - run commands
problems - see diagnostics
editFiles - modify files

For the full and up-to-date list, see code.visualstudio.com/docs/copilot/copilot-customization.
steps.tip: Restricting tools is how you control an agent's power level. A read-only reviewer might only get changes and codebase.

steps.title: Panels Closed
steps.instruction:
We've closed the editor tab for you.

For future reference:
• New File: Ctrl+N

steps.title: Use Your Code Reviewer
steps.instruction:
Open Copilot Chat. At the top of the chat panel, click the chat mode selector dropdown (where it shows the current mode like Ask, Edit, or Agent). Your code-reviewer agent should appear in the list.

Select it and ask:

Review the prompt and instruction files I've created in this lab.

Watch how the agent applies its specialized persona — it should give structured feedback with severity levels.

steps.title: Switch to the Docs Writer
steps.instruction:
Open the chat mode selector dropdown again and select your docs-writer agent. Ask:

Write a README section explaining the customization files in our .github folder.

Notice the difference in tone and output structure compared to the code reviewer. Same codebase, different perspective — that's the power of specialized agents.

steps.title: Chain Primitives Together
steps.instruction:
Now combine what you've learned. Run your generate-test prompt file (type / in chat), then switch to your code-reviewer agent via the mode selector dropdown and ask it to review the generated tests.

This is the full composition: Prompts trigger. Instructions guide. Skills inform. Agents execute.
steps.tip: You've just experienced the entire customization model. Every piece is a Markdown file committed to your repo.

steps.title: Panels Closed
steps.instruction:
We've closed the sidebar for you.

For future reference:
• Copilot Chat: Ctrl+Shift+I

steps.title: Agents Are the New Chat Modes
steps.instruction:
The .agent.md format is the unified way to create both custom agents and custom chat modes. If you define an agent with restricted tools and a focused workflow, that acts as a custom mode. If you give it broad tool access and a specialized role, that acts as a custom agent.

Same file format, both use cases.

steps.title: Legacy: .chatmode.md
steps.instruction:
You may encounter references to a legacy format called .chatmode.md. This was the original file format for defining custom chat modes in VS Code. It is still supported for backwards compatibility, but the .agent.md format has replaced it entirely.

If you have existing .chatmode.md files, they will continue to work, but new customization files should always use the .agent.md extension.
steps.tip: If you inherit a repo with .chatmode.md files, consider renaming them to .agent.md. The frontmatter and body format are compatible.

steps.title: Explore Copilot Memory
steps.instruction:
Copilot has a persistent memory system with three scopes. Ask Copilot in chat:

What do you currently have in your memory?

Copilot will show you any existing user, repository, or session memories. If this is a fresh workspace, memory may be empty.

steps.title: Create a User Memory
steps.instruction:
User memory persists across all workspaces and conversations. Tell Copilot:

Remember that I prefer Vitest over Jest for testing, and I always want TypeScript strict mode enabled.

Copilot stores this preference. From now on, it will factor this into responses in every project you work on.

steps.title: Create a Repository Memory
steps.instruction:
Repository memory is scoped to the current workspace. Tell Copilot:

Remember that this project uses Express for the API, Prisma for the database, and deploys with azd up.

This fact is stored with the workspace. This fact persists across sessions for you in this workspace. To share context with the team, use committed instruction files instead.

steps.title: Memory vs. Instructions
steps.instruction:
Ask Copilot:

What is the difference between memory and instruction files?

Key insight: Instructions are explicit rules you write in files and commit to your repo. Memory is knowledge Copilot accumulates through working with you. Instructions say 'always do X.' Memory says 'this developer prefers Y' or 'this project uses Z.' They complement each other.
steps.tip: Session memory is the third scope. It stores task-specific notes for the current conversation only and clears when the session ends.

steps.title: Panels Closed
steps.instruction:
We've closed the sidebar for you.

For future reference:
• Copilot Chat: Ctrl+Shift+I

steps.title: Lab Complete!
steps.instruction:
Congratulations! 🎉 You've created all four Copilot customization primitives and explored memory:

✅ Prompt files — generate-test and document-function
✅ Instruction files — repo-wide standards, test-scoped rules, and component-scoped rules
✅ Skills — API design and database pattern knowledge
✅ Agents — a code reviewer and a documentation writer
✅ Memory — user preferences and repository context

Commit these files to your repo and your entire team gets the same AI-powered workflow from day one.
steps.tip: Next up: Agent Potato: License to Chat — put these primitives to work building a full-stack app.
